---
title: "Stepbystep"
lang: en
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
filters:
  - stepbystep
---

**Stepbystep** is an extension for [Quarto](https://quarto.org/) that provides tools for organizing step-by-step tasks, tracking exercise progress, and visually styling instructions with image element highlighting.


## Installation

Add the extension to your Quarto project:

```bash
quarto add skyfroger/stepbystep
```

After installation, shortcodes and filters will be available once `stepbystep` is connected to the project or a specific page (as in the YAML header of this document).


## Task Steps and Self-Study

### Action Blocks (`sbsaction`)

To format a set of actions as a single instruction step, wrap each action in a block with the `.sbsaction` class.

**Demo:**

:::{.sbsaction}
First action.
:::

Explanations

:::{.sbsaction}
Second action.
:::

Further explanations

### Self-Study Blocks (`sbstask`)

Tasks for independent completion are placed inside a block with the `.sbstask` class.

**Demo:**

:::{.sbstask}
What needs to be done independently.
:::

### Progress Tracking

Each `.sbsaction` and `.sbstask` block has a checkbox that the learner can use to mark a completed step. This helps track task progress and avoid confusion.

### Reset Button (`sbsreset`)

The `sbsreset` shortcode adds a button that removes all checkmarks from completed blocks.

**Syntax:**

```markdown
{{{< sbsreset >}}}
```

**Demo:**

{{< sbsreset >}}


## Image Element Highlighting

The extension allows you to create interactive links to image elements with highlighting of corresponding areas on hover.

### Shortcodes `element` and `pin`

These shortcodes create styling elements that point to highlighted areas of an image.

**Syntax:**

```markdown
{{{< element name hl="id" >}}}
{{{< pin number hl="id" >}}}
```

**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| *(first argument)* | Element name or pin number. |
| `hl` | ID of the element to highlight. Optional parameter. |

**Demo:**

Connect the {{< element photoresistor hl="ldr" >}} with one of its contacts to pin {{< pin 14 hl="pin14-02" >}}.

### Highlight Container (`hl-container`)

The image whose elements need to be highlighted is placed in a block with the `.hl-container` class. Inside the container, `hl` shortcodes are placed that define the highlight areas.

**Shortcode `hl`:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| *(first argument)* | ID of the highlight element (must match the `hl` parameter in the `element` or `pin` shortcode). |
| `pos` | Values of the `top`, `left`, `width`, and `height` attributes, separated by spaces. Specified in percentages. |

**Demo:**

:::{.hl-container}

![](images/img.png)

{{< hl pin14-02 pos="39 75.3 3 5" >}}

{{< hl ldr pos="56 22.3 18 11" >}}

:::


### Full Markup Example

```markdown
Connect the {{{< element photoresistor hl="ldr" >}}} with one of its contacts to pin {{{< pin 14 hl="pin14-02" >}}}. 

:::{.hl-container}

![](images/img.png)

{{{< hl pin14-02 pos="39 75.3 3 5" >}}}

{{{< hl ldr pos="56 22.3 18 11" >}}}

:::
```

:::{.callout-caution title="Identifiers"}
Identifier names in the `hl` parameter and in the corresponding shortcode must be unique. Otherwise, arrows and highlighting will not work correctly.
:::

If you hover over an element, pin, or image element, an arrow will appear connecting the text to the area on the image.

## Hotspot (Interactive Points)

The `hs` shortcode allows you to add an active point with a tooltip popup to an image.

**Syntax:**

```markdown
{{{< hs "Tooltip text" left top marker="1" >}}}
```

**Parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| *(first argument)* | Tooltip text. |
| `left` | Relative offset from the left edge of the image (in percentages). |
| `top` | Relative offset from the top edge of the image (in percentages). |
| `marker` | Text inside the marker. The value must be enclosed in **quotes**. |

**Demo:**

:::: {.hl-container}
![](images/img.png)

{{< hs "LED with a current-limiting resistor." 73 66 marker="1">}}

{{< hs "The photoresistor changes its resistance depending on the light level." 19.5 55 >}}

::: {.sbshs left="45" top="20" marker="2"}
The following code will turn on the LED:

```python
from machine import Pin
led = Pin(12, Pin.OUT)
led.on()
```
:::
::::

### Hotspot Markup with Code (`sbshs`)

A block with the `.sbshs` class allows you to place arbitrary markup inside a hotspot, including code blocks. Used inside `.hl-container`.

**Block `.sbshs` parameters:**

| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `left` | Left offset (in percentages). |
| `top` | Top offset (in percentages). |
| `marker` | Text inside the marker. |

**Full Markup Example:**

````markdown
:::{.hl-container}

![](images/img.png)

{{{< hs "LED with a current-limiting resistor." 73 66 marker="1" >}}}

{{{< hs "The photoresistor changes its resistance depending on the light level." 19.5 55 >}}}

:::{.sbshs left=45 top=20 marker=2}

The following code will turn on the LED:

```python
from machine import Pin
led = Pin(12, Pin.OUT)
led.on()
```

:::

:::
````

## Page-by-Page Tutorial

The `.pagebypage` block splits instructions into slides. Each slide starts with a heading. Navigation is available via the menu and buttons.

::::{.pagebypage}

### Welcome to Python

Python is a powerful and easy-to-learn programming language. In this tutorial, you will write your first calculator program.

:::{.sbsaction}
Open the code editor below and read the sample code carefully.


```python
# This is a comment - Python ignores it
print("Hello, Python!")
```

:::

### Variables and Numbers

Variables store data for later use. In Python, you create a variable by assigning a value with =.

:::{.sbsaction}
Study how variables a and b store numbers:

```python
a = 15
b = 4

print("a =", a)
print("b =", b)
```

:::

:::{.sbsaction}
Python supports standard arithmetic operations:

```python
a = 15
b = 4

print("Sum:", a + b)        # Addition
print("Difference:", a - b)  # Subtraction
print("Product:", a * b)     # Multiplication
print("Quotient:", a / b)    # Division
print("Power:", a ** b)      # Exponentiation
```
:::

:::{.sbstask}
Change the values of a and b to 20 and 3, then run the code again.
:::

### User Input

The input() function asks the user to enter data. By default, it returns a string.

:::{.sbsaction}
Run this code and enter your name when prompted:

```python
name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Hello,", name)
```
:::

:::{.sbsaction}
For numbers, convert the input using int() or float():

```python
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
print("Next year you will be", age + 1)
```
:::

:::{.sbstask}
Write a program that asks for two numbers and prints their sum.
:::


### Building the Calculator

Now combine everything into a simple calculator.

:::{.sbsaction}
This calculator adds two numbers entered by the user:

```python
num1 = float(input("Enter first number: "))
num2 = float(input("Enter second number: "))

result = num1 + num2
print("Result:", result)
```
:::

:::{.sbsaction}
Extend the calculator to support all four operations:

```python
num1 = float(input("Enter first number: "))
num2 = float(input("Enter second number: "))
operation = input("Choose operation (+, -, *, /): ")

if operation == "+":
    result = num1 + num2
elif operation == "-":
    result = num1 - num2
elif operation == "*":
    result = num1 * num2
elif operation == "/":
    result = num1 / num2
else:
    result = "Unknown operation"

print("Result:", result)
```
:::

:::{.sbstask}
Add exponentiation (**) and integer division (//) to the calculator.
:::

### Challenge: BMI Calculator

Test your skills with a practical task.

:::{.sbstask}
Write a BMI (Body Mass Index) calculator:

1. Ask the user for weight in kilograms.
2. Ask the user for height in meters.
3. Calculate BMI using the formula: BMI = weight / (height ** 2)
4. Print the result rounded to 2 decimal places.

Use round(value, 2) to round the number.
:::

::::

## Markup Tips

1. **Identifier uniqueness.** Names in the `hl` parameter of the `element`, `pin`, and `hl` shortcodes must be unique. Duplication will cause arrows and highlighting to malfunction.
2. **Percentage coordinates.** The `pos` parameter in the `hl` shortcode and the `left`/`top` attributes in `hs`/`sbshs` are specified as percentages relative to the image dimensions. This ensures responsiveness when scaling.
3. **Quotes for marker.** The `marker` parameter value in the `hs` shortcode must be enclosed in quotes, even if it is a number.
4. **Container nesting.** The `.hl-container` block can contain both `hl` shortcodes and `hs`/`sbshs`. For `sbshs` with code, use 4 colons (`::::`) for the outer block to avoid conflicts with inner blocks of 3 colons (`:::`).
5. **Filter in YAML.** Don't forget to add `filters: - stepbystep` to the document header, otherwise shortcodes and blocks will not work.
